subject: Physics
topic: Atomic Masses and Composition of Nucleus
text: Atomic Number: The number of protons in the nucleus is called the atomic number. It is denoted by Z.
text_html: Atomic Number: The number of protons in the nucleus is called the atomic number. It is denoted by Z.

subject: Physics
topic: Atomic Masses and Composition of Nucleus
text: Mass number: The total number of protons and neutrons present in a nucleus is called the mass number of the element. It is denoted by A. No. of Protons, Electrons, Nucleons, and Neutrons in an Atom: Number of protons in an atom = Z Number of electrons in an atom = Z Number of nucleons in an atom = A Number of neutrons in an atom = N = A - Z
text_html:
Mass number: The total number of protons and neutrons present in a nucleus is called the mass number of the element. It is denoted by A.
No. of Protons, Electrons, Nucleons, and Neutrons in an Atom:
Number of protons in an atom = Z
Number of electrons in an atom = Z
Number of nucleons in an atom = A
Number of neutrons in an atom = N = A - Z

subject: Physics
topic: Atomic Masses and Composition of Nucleus
text: Nuclear Mass: The total mass of the protons and neutrons present in a nucleus is called the nuclear mass.
text_html: Nuclear Mass: The total mass of the protons and neutrons present in a nucleus is called the nuclear mass.

subject: Physics
topic: Atomic Masses and Composition of Nucleus
text: Nuclide: A nuclide is a specific nucleus of an atom characterized by its atomic number Z and mass number A. It is represented as, Z X A
text_html: Nuclide: A nuclide is a specific nucleus of an atom characterized by its atomic number Z and mass number A. It is represented as, ZXA

subject: Physics
topic: Atomic Masses and Composition of Nucleus
text: Atomic Mass Unit: It is 121th of the actual mass of a carbon atom of isotope 6C 12 . It is denoted by amu or just by u am u = 1.660565 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 kg
text_html:
Atomic Mass Unit:
It is 121th of the actual mass of a carbon atom of isotope 6C12. It is denoted by amu or just by u am u = 1.660565 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-27 kg

subject: Physics
topic: Atomic Masses and Composition of Nucleus
text: The energy equivalence of 1 amu is 1 amu = 931 MeV
text_html: The energy equivalence of 1 amu is 1 amu = 931 MeV

subject: Physics
topic: Atomic Masses and Composition of Nucleus
text: Size of the Nucleus: It is found that a nucleus of mass number A has a radius R = R o {tex}A^\frac {1}{3}{/tex} Where, R 0 - 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15 m his implies that the volume of the nucleus, which is proportional to R 3 is proportional to A.
text_html:
Size of the Nucleus:

  1. It is found that a nucleus of mass number A has a radius
    R = Ro{tex}A^\frac {1}{3}{/tex} Where, R0 - 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-15 m
  2. his implies that the volume of the nucleus, which is proportional to R3 is proportional to A.

subject: Physics
topic: Atomic Masses and Composition of Nucleus
text: Atomic Mass Unit (amu) The mass of an atom is minuscule compared to a kilogram. To measure such small quantities, the atomic mass unit (u) is used, defined as 1/12 of the mass of a carbon-12 atom. Its value is approximately 1.660539 x 10^-27 kg.
text_html:
Atomic Mass Unit (amu)
The mass of an atom is minuscule compared to a kilogram. To measure such small quantities, the atomic mass unit (u) is used, defined as 1/12 of the mass of a carbon-12 atom. Its value is approximately 1.660539 x 10^-27 kg.

subject: Physics
topic: Atomic Masses and Composition of Nucleus
text: Atomic Mass of Elements Atomic masses of elements expressed in atomic mass unit (u) often align closely with integral multiples of hydrogen's mass. However, exceptions like chlorine, with an atomic mass of 35.46 u, exist.
text_html:
Atomic Mass of Elements
Atomic masses of elements expressed in atomic mass unit (u) often align closely with integral multiples of hydrogen's mass. However, exceptions like chlorine, with an atomic mass of 35.46 u, exist.

subject: Physics
topic: Atomic Masses and Composition of Nucleus
text: Role of Mass Spectrometer Mass spectrometers accurately measure atomic masses and reveal isotopes, atoms of the same element that share chemical properties but vary in mass.
text_html:
Role of Mass Spectrometer
Mass spectrometers accurately measure atomic masses and reveal isotopes, atoms of the same element that share chemical properties but vary in mass.

subject: Physics
topic: Atomic Masses and Composition of Nucleus
text: Isotopes Isotopes of an element side-by-side in the periodic table have different masses. Isotopes like chlorine have varying relative abundances; its isotopes weigh 34.98 u and 36.98 u at 75.4% and 24.6% relative abundances.
text_html:
Isotopes
Isotopes of an element side-by-side in the periodic table have different masses. Isotopes like chlorine have varying relative abundances; its isotopes weigh 34.98 u and 36.98 u at 75.4% and 24.6% relative abundances.

subject: Physics
topic: Atomic Masses and Composition of Nucleus
text: Hydrogen Isotopes Hydrogen has three isotopes: proton (1.0078 u), deuterium (2.0141 u), and tritium (3.0160 u). Tritium is unstable and artificially produced. The proton's mass equals hydrogen's atomic mass minus the electron's mass.
text_html:
Hydrogen Isotopes
Hydrogen has three isotopes: proton (1.0078 u), deuterium (2.0141 u), and tritium (3.0160 u). Tritium is unstable and artificially produced. The proton's mass equals hydrogen's atomic mass minus the electron's mass.

subject: Physics
topic: Atomic Masses and Composition of Nucleus
text: Discovery of Neutron James Chadwick discovered the neutron in 1932, proving the existence of neutral matter within atomic nuclei by bombarding beryllium with alpha particles. This discovery earned him the 1935 Nobel Prize in Physics.
text_html:
Discovery of Neutron
James Chadwick discovered the neutron in 1932, proving the existence of neutral matter within atomic nuclei by bombarding beryllium with alpha particles. This discovery earned him the 1935 Nobel Prize in Physics.

subject: Physics
topic: Atomic Masses and Composition of Nucleus
text: Proton and Neutron Masses The proton's mass is 1.67262 x 10^-27 kg, whereas a neutron is slightly heavier at 1.6749 x 10^-27 kg.
text_html:
Proton and Neutron Masses
The proton's mass is 1.67262 x 10^-27 kg, whereas a neutron is slightly heavier at 1.6749 x 10^-27 kg.

subject: Physics
topic: Atomic Masses and Composition of Nucleus
text: Atomic Composition Atoms consist of protons and neutrons (nucleons). The atomic number (Z) is the number of protons, the neutron number (N) is the number of neutrons, and the mass number (A) totals protons and neutrons.
text_html:
Atomic Composition
Atoms consist of protons and neutrons (nucleons). The atomic number (Z) is the number of protons, the neutron number (N) is the number of neutrons, and the mass number (A) totals protons and neutrons.

subject: Physics
topic: Atomic Masses and Composition of Nucleus
text: Nuclear Notation Nuclides are denoted with the notation _Z^A X, where Z is the atomic number, A is the mass number, and X is the chemical symbol. For example, gold is represented as _79^197 Au, having 197 nucleons.
text_html:
Nuclear Notation
Nuclides are denoted with the notation _Z^A X, where Z is the atomic number, A is the mass number, and X is the chemical symbol. For example, gold is represented as _79^197 Au, having 197 nucleons.

subject: Physics
topic: Atomic Masses and Composition of Nucleus
text: Isobars and Isotones Isobars are nuclides with the same mass number, such as _1^3 H and _2^3 He. Isotones differ by atomic number but have the same neutron number, like _80^198 Hg and _79^197 Au.
text_html:
Isobars and Isotones
Isobars are nuclides with the same mass number, such as _1^3 H and _2^3 He. Isotones differ by atomic number but have the same neutron number, like _80^198 Hg and _79^197 Au.

subject: Physics
text: Rutherford's Pioneering Work Rutherford postulated the existence of the atomic nucleus and laid the groundwork with his experiments.
text_html: Rutherford's Pioneering Work Rutherford postulated the existence of the atomic nucleus and laid the groundwork with his experiments.

subject: Physics
text: Geiger-Marsden Experiment They conducted experiments on the scattering of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles by thin gold foils.
text_html: Geiger-Marsden Experiment They conducted experiments on the scattering of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles by thin gold foils.

subject: Physics
text: Results of Scattering Experiment • {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles of kinetic energy 5.5 MeV showed a closest approach of about {tex}4.0 \times 10^{-14} {~m}{/tex} to the gold nucleus. • The scattering was due to coulomb repulsion.
text_html: Results of Scattering Experiment • {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles of kinetic energy 5.5 MeV showed a closest approach of about {tex}4.0 \times 10^{-14} {~m}{/tex} to the gold nucleus. • The scattering was due to coulomb repulsion.

subject: Physics
text: Size and Forces at Play Nucleus size is less than {tex}4.0 \times 10^{-14} {~m}{/tex} . High-energy {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles may reveal short-range nuclear forces affecting scattering.
text_html: Size and Forces at Play Nucleus size is less than {tex}4.0 \times 10^{-14} {~m}{/tex}. High-energy {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles may reveal short-range nuclear forces affecting scattering.

subject: Physics
text: Implications of Multiple Experiments Experiments with fast electrons provided accurate measurements of nuclei sizes across various elements.
text_html: Implications of Multiple Experiments Experiments with fast electrons provided accurate measurements of nuclei sizes across various elements.

subject: Physics
text: Nucleus Radius Calculation The formula $$R=R_0 A^{1/3}$$ was derived, with $$R_0 = 1.2 \times 10^{-15} {~m}$$, defining the radius of a nucleus.
text_html: Nucleus Radius Calculation The formula $$R=R_0 A^{1/3}$$ was derived, with $$R_0 = 1.2 \times 10^{-15} {~m}$$, defining the radius of a nucleus.

subject: Physics
text: Nucleus Volume and Density • Nucleus volume is proportional to {tex}A{/tex} . • Density remains constant across nuclei, around {tex}2.3 \times 10^{17} {~kg} {~m}^{-3}{/tex} .
text_html: Nucleus Volume and Density • Nucleus volume is proportional to {tex}A{/tex}. • Density remains constant across nuclei, around {tex}2.3 \times 10^{17} {~kg} {~m}^{-3}{/tex}.

subject: Physics
text: Nucleus as a Liquid Drop Different nuclei can be compared to a drop of liquid with constant density, different from the large empty spaces in ordinary atoms.
text_html: Nucleus as a Liquid Drop Different nuclei can be compared to a drop of liquid with constant density, different from the large empty spaces in ordinary atoms.

subject: Physics
text: Comparative Densities Nuclear density is much greater than that of water, which is {tex}10^3 {~kg} {~m}^{-3}{/tex} .
text_html: Comparative Densities Nuclear density is much greater than that of water, which is {tex}10^3 {~kg} {~m}^{-3}{/tex}.

subject: Physics
text: Conclusion on Atomic Structure Understanding of nuclear structure clarifies that atoms consist mostly of empty space.
text_html: Conclusion on Atomic Structure Understanding of nuclear structure clarifies that atoms consist mostly of empty space.

subject: Physics
text: Electron Volt: It is defined as the energy acquired by an electron when it is accelerated through a potential difference of 1 volt and is denoted by eV.
text_html: Electron Volt: It is defined as the energy acquired by an electron when it is accelerated through a potential difference of 1 volt and is denoted by eV.

subject: Physics
text: Mass Defect: The difference between the rest mass of a nucleus and the sum of the rest masses of its constituent nucleons is called its mass defect. It is given by- {tex}\Delta{/tex} m = [Zm p + (A - Z)m n ] - m
text_html:
Mass Defect: The difference between the rest mass of a nucleus and the sum of the rest masses of its constituent nucleons is called its mass defect. It is given by-
{tex}\Delta{/tex}m = [Zmp + (A - Z)mn] - m

subject: Physics
text: Binding Energy: It may be defined as the energy required to break a nucleus into its constituent protons and neutrons and to separate them to such a large distance that they may not interact with each other. It may also be defined as the surplus energy which the nucleus gives up by virtue of their attractions which they become bound together to form a nucleus. The binding energy of a nucleus Z X A is- B.E. = [Zm p + (A - Z) m n - m]c 2
text_html:
Binding Energy:

  1. It may be defined as the energy required to break a nucleus into its constituent protons and neutrons and to separate them to such a large distance that they may not interact with each other.
  2. It may also be defined as the surplus energy which the nucleus gives up by virtue of their attractions which they become bound together to form a nucleus.
  3. The binding energy of a nucleus ZXA is-
    B.E. = [Zmp + (A - Z) mn - m]c2

subject: Physics
text: Binding Energy per Nucleon: It is average energy required to extract one nucleon from the nucleus. It is obtained by dividing the binding energy of a nucleus by its mass number. Binding Energy Curve
text_html:
Binding Energy per Nucleon: It is average energy required to extract one nucleon from the nucleus. It is obtained by dividing the binding energy of a nucleus by its mass number.
Binding Energy Curve

subject: Physics
text: Main features of the curve are given below: The binding energy per nucleon (Ebn) is practically constant, i.e. practically independent of the atomic number for nuclei of middle mass number (30 < A < 170). The curve has a maximum of about 8.75 MeV for A = 56 and has a value of 7.6 MeV for A = 238. Binding energy per nucleon is lower for both light nuclei (A < 30) and heavy nuclei (A > 170).
text_html:
Main features of the curve are given below:

  1. The binding energy per nucleon (Ebn) is practically constant, i.e. practically independent of the atomic number for nuclei of middle mass number (30 < A < 170). The curve has a maximum of about 8.75 MeV for A = 56 and has a value of 7.6 MeV for A = 238.
  2. Binding energy per nucleon is lower for both light nuclei (A < 30) and heavy nuclei (A > 170).

subject: Physics
text: **Mass-Energy Equivalence** Einstein showed that mass is a form of energy, according to special relativity. This means that mass and energy can be converted into each other, known as the mass-energy equivalence.
text_html:
**Mass-Energy Equivalence**
Einstein showed that mass is a form of energy, according to special relativity. This means that mass and energy can be converted into each other, known as the mass-energy equivalence.

subject: Physics
text: **Mass-Energy Relation** The famous equation that relates mass and energy is {tex} E=mc^2 {/tex} . In this equation, {tex} c {/tex} represents the speed of light, approximately {tex} 3 \times 10^8 {~ms}^{-1} {/tex} .
text_html:
**Mass-Energy Relation**
The famous equation that relates mass and energy is {tex} E=mc^2 {/tex}. In this equation, {tex} c {/tex} represents the speed of light, approximately {tex} 3 \times 10^8 {~ms}^{-1} {/tex}.

subject: Physics
text: **Conservation of Energy in Reactions** Before special relativity, mass and energy were presumed to be conserved separately. Einstein showed that the energy conservation law includes mass-energy, ensuring initial and final energies are equal.
text_html:
**Conservation of Energy in Reactions**
Before special relativity, mass and energy were presumed to be conserved separately. Einstein showed that the energy conservation law includes mass-energy, ensuring initial and final energies are equal.

subject: Physics
text: **Experimental Verification** Einstein's theory has been tested through nuclear reactions where nucleons, nuclei, and other particles are involved, providing proof for the mass-energy equivalence.
text_html:
**Experimental Verification**
Einstein's theory has been tested through nuclear reactions where nucleons, nuclei, and other particles are involved, providing proof for the mass-energy equivalence.

subject: Physics
text: **Nuclear Binding Energy** Nuclear mass is less than the sum of its protons and neutrons. This mass difference, known as mass defect, relates to the binding energy that holds the nucleus together.
text_html:
**Nuclear Binding Energy**
Nuclear mass is less than the sum of its protons and neutrons. This mass difference, known as mass defect, relates to the binding energy that holds the nucleus together.

subject: Physics
text: **Mass Defect and Energy** The mass defect, {tex} \Delta M {/tex} , is converted to energy using {tex} E_b = \Delta M c^2 {/tex} , which is the binding energy of the nucleus.
text_html:
**Mass Defect and Energy**
The mass defect, {tex} \Delta M {/tex}, is converted to energy using {tex} E_b = \Delta M c^2 {/tex}, which is the binding energy of the nucleus.

subject: Physics
text: **Binding Energy Per Nucleon** The binding energy per nucleon, {tex} E_{bn} {/tex} , is the average energy needed to separate a nucleus into individual nucleons. It helps measure the stability of the nucleus.
text_html:
**Binding Energy Per Nucleon**
The binding energy per nucleon, {tex} E_{bn} {/tex}, is the average energy needed to separate a nucleus into individual nucleons. It helps measure the stability of the nucleus.

subject: Physics
text: **Binding Energy Characteristics** The plot shows {tex} E_{bn} {/tex} is constant for nuclei with {tex} 30 {/tex} E_{bn} {tex}{/tex} decreases for very light and very heavy nuclei.
text_html:
**Binding Energy Characteristics**

  • The plot shows {tex} E_{bn} {/tex} is constant for nuclei with {tex} 30
  • {/tex} E_{bn} {tex}{/tex} decreases for very light and very heavy nuclei.

subject: Physics
text: **Nuclear Force Properties** The nuclear force is short-ranged, affecting only nearby nucleons, resulting in consistent binding energy per nucleon for larger nuclei. This phenomenon is known as the saturation property of nuclear force.
text_html:
**Nuclear Force Properties**
The nuclear force is short-ranged, affecting only nearby nucleons, resulting in consistent binding energy per nucleon for larger nuclei. This phenomenon is known as the saturation property of nuclear force.

subject: Physics
text: **Implications in Energy Production** Heavier nuclei like {tex} A=240 {/tex} have lower {tex} E_{bn} {/tex} compared to {tex} A=120 {/tex} . In fission, a nucleus splitting releases energy due to increased {tex} E_{bn} {/tex} of the products. Fusion processes in the sun release energy as lighter nuclei fuse to form more tightly bound heavier nuclei.
text_html:
**Implications in Energy Production**

  • Heavier nuclei like {tex} A=240 {/tex} have lower {tex} E_{bn} {/tex} compared to {tex} A=120 {/tex}.
  • In fission, a nucleus splitting releases energy due to increased {tex} E_{bn} {/tex} of the products.
  • Fusion processes in the sun release energy as lighter nuclei fuse to form more tightly bound heavier nuclei.

subject: Physics
text: Density of the Nucleus: Density of nucleus is constant; independent of A, for all nuclei and density of nuclear matter is approximately 2.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 17 kgm -3 which is very large as compared to ordinary matter, say water which is 103 kg m -3 . Mass-Energy equivalence: Einstein proved that it is necessary to treat mass as another form of energy. He gave the mass-energy equivalence relation as, E = mc 2 Where m is the mass and c is the velocity of light in vacuum. Nuclear density {tex}\rho_v=\frac{\text { Nuclear mass }}{\text { Nuclear volume }}{/tex}
text_html:
Density of the Nucleus: Density of nucleus is constant; independent of A, for all nuclei and density of nuclear matter is approximately 2.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1017 kgm-3 which is very large as compared to ordinary matter, say water which is 103 kg m-3.
Mass-Energy equivalence: Einstein proved that it is necessary to treat mass as another form of energy. He gave the mass-energy equivalence relation as, E = mc2 Where m is the mass and c is the velocity of light in vacuum.
Nuclear density {tex}\rho_v=\frac{\text { Nuclear mass }}{\text { Nuclear volume }}{/tex}

subject: Physics
text: Nuclear Forces: Nuclear force is a strong fundamental force in nature. Nuclear force is mainly an attractive force: Nuclear force is charge independent. Nuclear force is short-range force. Nuclear force is a saturated force. Nuclear force is an exchange force. Nuclear force is non-central force. Nuclear force is spin-dependent.
text_html:
Nuclear Forces:

  1. Nuclear force is a strong fundamental force in nature.
  2. Nuclear force is mainly an attractive force: Nuclear force is charge independent.
  3. Nuclear force is short-range force.
  4. Nuclear force is a saturated force.
  5. Nuclear force is an exchange force.
  6. Nuclear force is non-central force.
  7. Nuclear force is spin-dependent.

subject: Physics
text: Nuclear Force vs Coulomb Force Nuclear force is significantly stronger than the Coulomb force that acts between charges. It overcomes the repulsive force between protons in the nucleus.
text_html:
Nuclear Force vs Coulomb Force
Nuclear force is significantly stronger than the Coulomb force that acts between charges. It overcomes the repulsive force between protons in the nucleus.

subject: Physics
text: Saturation of Nuclear Force The potential energy between nucleons drops to zero when their separation extends beyond a few femtometers. This results in a constant binding energy per nucleon for medium and large nuclei.
text_html:
Saturation of Nuclear Force
The potential energy between nucleons drops to zero when their separation extends beyond a few femtometers. This results in a constant binding energy per nucleon for medium and large nuclei.

subject: Physics
text: Nucleon Separation and Potential Energy Potential energy is minimized at a nucleon separation of approximately 0.8 fm. Forces are attractive at distances larger than 0.8 fm and become repulsive closer than 0.8 fm.
text_html:
Nucleon Separation and Potential Energy
Potential energy is minimized at a nucleon separation of approximately 0.8 fm. Forces are attractive at distances larger than 0.8 fm and become repulsive closer than 0.8 fm.

subject: Physics
text: Consistency in Nuclear Force The nuclear force remains about the same across different nucleon pairings: neutron-neutron, proton-neutron, and proton-proton.
text_html:
Consistency in Nuclear Force
The nuclear force remains about the same across different nucleon pairings: neutron-neutron, proton-neutron, and proton-proton.

subject: Physics
text: Nature of Nuclear Force Nuclear force is unique compared to gravitational and electrostatic forces as it is independent of electric charge.
text_html:
Nature of Nuclear Force
Nuclear force is unique compared to gravitational and electrostatic forces as it is independent of electric charge.

subject: Physics
text: Short-Range Nuclear Force Nuclear forces have a very short reach, with interaction between nucleons effectively ending a few femto-meters apart.
text_html:
Short-Range Nuclear Force
Nuclear forces have a very short reach, with interaction between nucleons effectively ending a few femto-meters apart.

subject: Physics
text: Binding Energy Per Nucleon Average binding energy for nucleons in a nucleus is around 8 MeV, much higher than typical atomic binding energies.
text_html:
Binding Energy Per Nucleon
Average binding energy for nucleons in a nucleus is around 8 MeV, much higher than typical atomic binding energies.

subject: Physics
text: Experimental Findings (1930-1950) Characteristics of nuclear forces were derived from various experiments conducted between the years 1930 and 1950.
text_html:
Experimental Findings (1930-1950)
Characteristics of nuclear forces were derived from various experiments conducted between the years 1930 and 1950.

subject: Physics
text: Nuclear and Gravitational Force Comparison Gravitational force is significantly weaker compared to both nuclear and Coulomb forces, impacting its irrelevance in nuclear binding.
text_html:
Nuclear and Gravitational Force Comparison
Gravitational force is significantly weaker compared to both nuclear and Coulomb forces, impacting its irrelevance in nuclear binding.

subject: Physics
text: No Simple Law for Nuclear Forces Unlike the predictable models for interactions through Coulomb's law or Newton's law, the nuclear force lacks a straightforward mathematical expression.
text_html:
No Simple Law for Nuclear Forces
Unlike the predictable models for interactions through Coulomb's law or Newton's law, the nuclear force lacks a straightforward mathematical expression.

subject: Physics
text: Nuclear Fission: It is the process in which a heavy nucleu smaller nuclei of nearly comparable masses. For example- {tex}{ }_{92}^{235} U+{ }_0^1 n \rightarrow{ }_{56}^{141} \mathrm{Ba}+{ }_{36}^{92} K r+3_0^1 n+Q{/tex}
text_html:
Nuclear Fission: It is the process in which a heavy nucleu smaller nuclei of nearly comparable masses. For example-
{tex}{ }_{92}^{235} U+{ }_0^1 n \rightarrow{ }_{56}^{141} \mathrm{Ba}+{ }_{36}^{92} K r+3_0^1 n+Q{/tex}

subject: Physics
text: Curve of Binding Energy The binding energy per nucleon (Eb n ) remains nearly constant at 8.0 MeV between mass numbers A=30 to A=170.
text_html: Curve of Binding Energy The binding energy per nucleon (Ebn) remains nearly constant at 8.0 MeV between mass numbers A=30 to A=170.

subject: Physics
text: Light and Heavy Nuclei The binding energy per nucleon decreases for light nuclei (A<30) and heavy nuclei (A>170).
text_html: Light and Heavy Nuclei The binding energy per nucleon decreases for light nuclei (A<30) and heavy nuclei (A>170).

subject: Physics
text: Energy Release in Nuclear Reactions Greater binding energy in a nucleus results in a lower total mass, thus energy is released when nuclei transform to configurations with higher binding energies. This occurs in both fission and fusion.
text_html: Energy Release in Nuclear Reactions Greater binding energy in a nucleus results in a lower total mass, thus energy is released when nuclei transform to configurations with higher binding energies. This occurs in both fission and fusion.

subject: Physics
text: Energy Comparison Exothermic chemical reactions release energies in electron volts, while nuclear reactions release energies in mega electron volts (MeV), making nuclear energy millions of times more potent than chemical energy.
text_html: Energy Comparison Exothermic chemical reactions release energies in electron volts, while nuclear reactions release energies in mega electron volts (MeV), making nuclear energy millions of times more potent than chemical energy.

subject: Physics
text: Fission Reaction Fission occurs when a heavy nucleus like Uranium-235 is bombarded with a neutron, resulting in the nucleus splitting into two or more smaller fragments and releasing energy.
text_html: Fission Reaction Fission occurs when a heavy nucleus like Uranium-235 is bombarded with a neutron, resulting in the nucleus splitting into two or more smaller fragments and releasing energy.

subject: Physics
text: Examples of Fission Reactions For instance, 235 U + n → 236 U → 144 Ba + 89 Kr + 3n 235 U + n → 236 U → 133 Sb + 99 Nb + 4n 235 U + n → 140 Xe + 94 Sr + 2n These reactions produce radioactive nuclei emitting β particles.
text_html:
Examples of Fission Reactions For instance,

  • 235U + n → 236U → 144Ba + 89Kr + 3n
  • 235U + n → 236U → 133Sb + 99Nb + 4n
  • 235U + n → 140Xe + 94Sr + 2n

These reactions produce radioactive nuclei emitting β particles.

subject: Physics
text: Energy Calculation in Fission The energy release (Q value) in nuclear fission reactions for nuclei such as Uranium is around 200 MeV per nucleus. For instance, when A=240 splits into fragments of A=120: Binding energy for A=240 is 7.6 MeV Binding energy per nucleon for A=120 is 8.5 MeV Total gain: 0.9 MeV per nucleon, i.e., 240 × 0.9 = 216 MeV
text_html:
Energy Calculation in Fission The energy release (Q value) in nuclear fission reactions for nuclei such as Uranium is around 200 MeV per nucleus. For instance, when A=240 splits into fragments of A=120:

  • Binding energy for A=240 is 7.6 MeV
  • Binding energy per nucleon for A=120 is 8.5 MeV
  • Total gain: 0.9 MeV per nucleon, i.e., 240 × 0.9 = 216 MeV

subject: Physics
text: Energy Distribution The energy from fission first appears as kinetic energy of fragments and neutrons, which is later transformed into heat, useful for various practical applications.
text_html: Energy Distribution The energy from fission first appears as kinetic energy of fragments and neutrons, which is later transformed into heat, useful for various practical applications.

subject: Physics
text: Nuclear Reactors The controlled energy release from nuclear fission is what powers nuclear reactors to produce electricity efficiently.
text_html: Nuclear Reactors The controlled energy release from nuclear fission is what powers nuclear reactors to produce electricity efficiently.

subject: Physics
text: Atomic Bombs The enormous destructive energy release in atomic bombs results from uncontrolled nuclear fission reactions.
text_html: Atomic Bombs The enormous destructive energy release in atomic bombs results from uncontrolled nuclear fission reactions.

subject: Physics
text: Nuclear Fusion: It is the process of fusion of two smaller nuclei into a heavier nucleus with the liberation of large amount of energy.
text_html: Nuclear Fusion: It is the process of fusion of two smaller nuclei into a heavier nucleus with the liberation of large amount of energy.

subject: Physics
text: nuclear Fusion and Energy Release When two light nuclei fuse to create a larger nucleus, energy is emitted due to the larger nucleus being more tightly bound, as illustrated in the binding energy curve.
text_html:
nuclear Fusion and Energy Release
When two light nuclei fuse to create a larger nucleus, energy is emitted due to the larger nucleus being more tightly bound, as illustrated in the binding energy curve.

subject: Physics
text: Examples of Fusion Reactions - Two protons combine to form a deuteron and a positron, causing a release of 0.42 MeV. - Two deuterons come together to create helium-3 and a neutron, emitting 3.27 MeV. - Two deuterons also form triton and a proton, releasing 4.03 MeV.
text_html:
Examples of Fusion Reactions
- Two protons combine to form a deuteron and a positron, causing a release of 0.42 MeV.
- Two deuterons come together to create helium-3 and a neutron, emitting 3.27 MeV.
- Two deuterons also form triton and a proton, releasing 4.03 MeV.

subject: Physics
text: Conditions for Fusion To achieve fusion, two nuclei need to be close enough for attractive nuclear force to act. Due to their positive charge, they experience Coulomb repulsion and need energy to surpass this barrier.
text_html:
Conditions for Fusion
To achieve fusion, two nuclei need to be close enough for attractive nuclear force to act. Due to their positive charge, they experience Coulomb repulsion and need energy to surpass this barrier.

subject: Physics
text: Overcoming the Coulomb Barrier - The barrier height for protons is around 400 keV, increasing with higher charges. - The temperature needed for protons to overcome this is approximately 3 million Kelvin.
text_html:
Overcoming the Coulomb Barrier
- The barrier height for protons is around 400 keV, increasing with higher charges.
- The temperature needed for protons to overcome this is approximately 3 million Kelvin.

subject: Physics
text: Thermonuclear Fusion Fusion achieved by increasing system temperature so particles overcome Coulomb repulsion is termed thermonuclear fusion, which fuels stars.
text_html:
Thermonuclear Fusion
Fusion achieved by increasing system temperature so particles overcome Coulomb repulsion is termed thermonuclear fusion, which fuels stars.

subject: Physics
text: Fusion in the Sun - The Sun's core temperature is 1.5 million Kelvin. - The proton-proton cycle in the Sun involves multiple fusion reactions converting hydrogen to helium.
text_html:
Fusion in the Sun
- The Sun's core temperature is 1.5 million Kelvin.
- The proton-proton cycle in the Sun involves multiple fusion reactions converting hydrogen to helium.

subject: Physics
text: Multi-step Reactions in the Sun Involve reactions such as: - Proton and proton form deuteron. - Positron and electron produce gamma rays. - Formation of helium-3 from deuteron and proton. - Two helium-3 create a helium-4 nucleus.
text_html:
Multi-step Reactions in the Sun
Involve reactions such as:
- Proton and proton form deuteron.
- Positron and electron produce gamma rays.
- Formation of helium-3 from deuteron and proton.
- Two helium-3 create a helium-4 nucleus.

subject: Physics
text: Total Reaction in the Sun E.g., four protons and two electrons merge, resulting in a helium atom, accompanied by the release of neutrinos, gamma rays, and energy.
text_html:
Total Reaction in the Sun
E.g., four protons and two electrons merge, resulting in a helium atom, accompanied by the release of neutrinos, gamma rays, and energy.

subject: Physics
text: Element Synthesis in Stars - Helium fuses into carbon when core temperatures soar to erupt fusion. - Heavier elements near binding energy peaks can form but not beyond.
text_html:
Element Synthesis in Stars
- Helium fuses into carbon when core temperatures soar to erupt fusion.
- Heavier elements near binding energy peaks can form but not beyond.

subject: Physics
text: Controlled Thermonuclear Fusion - Mimics natural fusion seen in stars to generate power. - Requires sustaining temperatures of 100 million Kelvin in reactors to establish plasma. - Global efforts, including India's, are directed at mastering this technology for endless power.
text_html:
Controlled Thermonuclear Fusion
- Mimics natural fusion seen in stars to generate power.
- Requires sustaining temperatures of 100 million Kelvin in reactors to establish plasma.
- Global efforts, including India's, are directed at mastering this technology for endless power.

subject: Physics
text: Nucleus Composition The nucleus of an atom holds the positive charge and most of the mass. The nucleus is densely concentrated at the atom's center.
text_html:
Nucleus Composition
The nucleus of an atom holds the positive charge and most of the mass.
  • The nucleus is densely concentrated at the atom's center.

subject: Physics
text: Nucleus vs Atom Size The nucleus is significantly smaller than the atom, specifically by a factor of about 10 -4 .
text_html:
Nucleus vs Atom Size
The nucleus is significantly smaller than the atom, specifically by a factor of about 10-4.

subject: Physics
text: Volume Comparison The volume of a nucleus is approximately 10 -12 times smaller than the volume of the atom.
text_html:
Volume Comparison
The volume of a nucleus is approximately 10-12 times smaller than the volume of the atom.

subject: Physics
text: Enlarged Atom Analogy If the atom is expanded to the size of a classroom, the nucleus would merely be the size of a pinhead.
text_html:
Enlarged Atom Analogy
If the atom is expanded to the size of a classroom, the nucleus would merely be the size of a pinhead.

subject: Physics
text: Nuclear Mass Concentration The nucleus contains more than 99.9% of an atom's total mass.
text_html:
Nuclear Mass Concentration
The nucleus contains more than 99.9% of an atom's total mass.

subject: Physics
text: Nuclear Size and Properties Nuclei have several properties — size, mass, and stability — to be discussed.
text_html:
Nuclear Size and Properties
Nuclei have several properties — size, mass, and stability — to be discussed.

subject: Physics
text: Radioactivity Radioactivity is a nuclear phenomenon involving the decay of nuclei leading to the emission of radiation.
text_html:
Radioactivity
Radioactivity is a nuclear phenomenon involving the decay of nuclei leading to the emission of radiation.

subject: Physics
text: Nuclear Fission Fission is a process where a nucleus splits into smaller parts, often producing free neutrons and photons (in the form of gamma rays).
text_html:
Nuclear Fission
Fission is a process where a nucleus splits into smaller parts, often producing free neutrons and photons (in the form of gamma rays).

subject: Physics
text: Nuclear Fusion Fusion is a process where two light nuclei combine to form a heavier nucleus, releasing a significant amount of energy.
text_html:
Nuclear Fusion
Fusion is a process where two light nuclei combine to form a heavier nucleus, releasing a significant amount of energy.

subject: Physics
text: Understanding Nuclei This chapter will explore the various questions surrounding nuclear size, mass, stability, and nuclear phenomena such as radioactivity, fission, and fusion.
text_html:
Understanding Nuclei
This chapter will explore the various questions surrounding nuclear size, mass, stability, and nuclear phenomena such as radioactivity, fission, and fusion.
